html:
How to use this guide

Read the briefing notes below before drilling the plans. They explain how the question list was generated, which characters and themes to rehearse first, what the seven-sentence body paragraph shape looks like, the three pedagogical depths Sophia threads into every Grade-9 answer, and the universal terminology you must memorise.

Overview

AQA GCSE English Literature — Lord of the Flies — 20 Most Likely Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's prediction model. The model combines six signals — frequency in past papers (lifted, because AQA recycles core topics rather than retiring them), cycle gap since last appearance, coverage gap (themes in the spec but never asked), unexplored-angle signal (sub-angles of an asked topic that remain unexplored), core-topic boost (the novella's three core themes + three core characters cannot be avoided forever), and a softened exact-angle-repeat penalty — across all Lord of the Flies questions 2017–2025. LotF differs from AIC: AQA does NOT strictly pair one character Q + one theme Q each year (2024 set two theme Qs; 2025 set two character Qs). This bank therefore presents two parallel sets of ten — ten CHARACTER Qs + ten THEME Qs — and AQA may select any combination. The the model update fixes a an earlier model iteration over-damping bug: an earlier model iteration would have buried Ralph and Jack at the bottom of the character ranking because they were asked at the joint relationship/change angle in 2025; the model's core-topic boost lifts core characters back to the top regardless of recency, while the softened ExactAngleRepeatPenalty fires only when a proposed 2026 angle matches the 2025 angle directly. The 2026 Paper 1 actuals (Macbeth = Guilt; R&J = Effect of Conflict; P&P = Elizabeth Bennet) confirmed the model's spine-first ranking pattern.

Note on section labels. Section IDs (C1–C10 + T1–T10) are stable character/theme IDs used throughout the cross-reference tables at the foot of the document; they are NOT priority order. The priority order under the model is given in the tables below.

Priority order — rehearse in this order

Characters (C1–C10 in priority order):

1 — C1 (Ralph): HIGH — Spine character + 5–6 unexplored angles. Required: prioritize fresh angles over `relationship with Jack / change` (asked 2025). Try `hamartia / decline of authority` / `hunted prey and the refused coming-of-age` / `elected chief's institutional failures`.

2 — C2 (Jack): HIGH — Spine character + many unexplored angles. Required: prioritize fresh angles over `relationship with Ralph / change` (asked 2025) and `change` standalone (asked 2020). Try `charisma and tyranny` / `choirboy to anti-protagonist` / `the painted face as institutional inversion`.

3 — C3 (Piggy): HIGH — Spine character + 5-year gap from 2021. Required: prioritize fresh angles over `relationship with Ralph` (asked 2021 joint). Try `reason institutionally disabled` / `scapegoat and class voice` / `keeper of the conch`.

4 — C4 (Roger): HIGH — Never asked in 9-year window + sadistic enforcer + unexplored-angle signal = 1.0.

5 — C5 (Simon): MEDIUM-HIGH — Asked 2025 at `goodness/morality` angle — fresh angle preferred. Try `prophet / messenger / killed dissent` / `mystic and visionary` / `christ-figure under religious-allegorical naming`.

6 — C6 (The Lord of the Flies (entity / voice)): MEDIUM — Never asked + sow's head as symbolic mouthpiece distinct from any character.

7 — C7 (Sam and Eric): MEDIUM — Never asked + conformity-under-tribal-pressure angle entirely open.

8 — C8 (The Naval Officer): MEDIUM — Never asked + deflationary-irony framing distinct from a War theme Q.

9 — C9 (The Littluns): MED-LOW — Asked 2024 at `trust/fear` angle — fresh angle preferred (try `childhood and adult violence` / `the disappeared mulberry-marked boy`).

10 — C10 (The Choir): LOW — Never asked; peripheral but pedagogically rich as the institutional inheritance Jack converts into the painted tribe.

Themes (T1–T10 in priority order):

1 — T1 (Civilisation vs Savagery): HIGH — Spine theme + 7-year gap from 2019 + the novella's defining thematic opposition.

2 — T2 (Inherent Evil / Good and Evil): HIGH — Spine theme + new angle (asked 2022 at `evil within every individual` — try `evil as institutional inheritance` / `good as the inheritance that fails to defend`).

3 — T3 (Loss of Innocence): HIGH — Spine theme + never asked verbatim + closing tricolon's most quoted sentence.

4 — T4 (Symbolism (the conch, the signal fire, the Lord of the Flies)): HIGH — Never asked + Golding's form-defining technical inheritance.

5 — T5 (Group Mentality / Mob / Chant): MEDIUM-HIGH — Never asked + Le Bon framework + dance and storm-killing.

6 — T6 (War / Adult War Frame): MEDIUM — Never asked + cruiser ending + atom bomb evacuation frame.

7 — T7 (Responsibility): MEDIUM — Never asked + Ralph's hamartia in three institutional registers.

8 — T8 (Fear and the Beast): MEDIUM — Asked 2018 + 2022 + 2024 at variant angles — fresh angle preferred (try `fear as projection of internal cruelty` / `the beast as institutional symbol`).

9 — T9 (Rules and Order): MEDIUM — Asked 2021 (5-year gap) + sibling theme of civilisation vs savagery.

10 — T10 (Violence and its Effects): MED-LOW — Asked 2023 (3-year gap) + the form's elegiac register a defensible new angle.

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10): any character + angle pair asked in 2024 or 2025 at the SAME angle proposed for 2026 — Littluns-as-trust-and-fear, Simon-as-goodness/morality, Ralph-and-Jack-as-changing-relationship, the leadership-and-power frame (asked 2024). These remain in the document for canonical coverage; do not rehearse them as 2026 predictions at those angles.

How to use this document

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 of each axis first (rows 1–5 in the tables above). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 20. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same scene-bank answers every Q here.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on Lord of the Flies should thread at least one of these readings into one BP or the conclusion. They are the novella's deepest political moves; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. The savagery on the island — universal-human-nature reading + imperial-British-public-school counter-reading.

The standard reading of Lord of the Flies identifies Golding's argument as universal anthropology: savagery lies under civilisation, the beast is internal, the boys' descent dramatises the inherited capacity for cruelty present in every human being. Golding served in the Royal Navy from 1940 to 1945, saw the Belsen liberation photographs in 1945, and drafted the novella between 1951 and 1953 with the death camps and the atomic destruction of Hiroshima and Nagasaki still inside living memory. Read this way, the painted face is not the loss of European civilisation — it is European civilisation. The beast is not on the mountain; it is in the boys, and through them, in every Belsen guard, every Enola Gay airman, every adult institutional executioner the post-war reader is being asked to recognise. Golding said in 1959 that the novella was "an attempt to trace the defects of society back to the defects of human nature".

The sharper counter-reading refuses the universal frame and specifies the cultural object. The boys are NOT a microcosm of humanity in general — they are a microcosm of PUBLIC-SCHOOL BRITISH boys, the product of an imperial educational system. The choirboys arrive "in two parallel lines... dressed in strangely eccentric clothing" with Jack as "head boy" and "chapter chorister"; Ralph reaches instinctively for the conch "like a man with the conch"; Piggy invokes "my auntie" and "asthma" as the inherited rhetoric of working-class deference; Roger emerges from "the school choir". The boys have been pre-trained inside the institutional violence of the imperial-British educational system — Empire Day pageants, cadet corps drill, public-school ritual, the inherited assumption that brown-skinned populations require British administration. The savagery they fall into is therefore not a universal regression but the SPECIFIC violence Britain had just enacted on a planetary scale through three centuries of empire and through World War II's strategic bombing of German and Japanese civilians. Read this way, "a pack of British boys" is the controlling object: the novella anatomises imperial-Britain's children, not humanity in general. The argument is sharper because it is specific. Thread this into: C1 Ralph / C2 Jack / C3 Piggy / C4 Roger / C10 The Choir / T1 Civilisation vs Savagery / T2 Inherent Evil / T3 Loss of Innocence / any essay with a Chapter 1 or Chapter 9 BP.

2. The naval officer ending — civilisation as savagery's more sophisticated form, NOT savagery's cure.

The novella's closing structural move is not the rescue itself but the institutional identity of the rescuer. The naval officer is a uniformed serviceman of the same Cold War whose nuclear evacuation produced the boys' plane crash in Chapter 1. The cruiser standing offshore is the boys' painted tribe scaled up by three orders of magnitude — same hierarchy, same ritualised uniform, same chosen violence, same institutional permission for the killing. When the officer says "Like the Coral Island" and "I should have thought that a pack of British boys — you're all British, aren't you? — would have been able to put up a better show than that", Golding closes the trap: the man whose institutional uniform makes him the boys' rescuer is the man whose institutional uniform makes him an executioner on a planetary scale, and he does not recognise what he is looking at because what he is looking at is what he himself is. The deflationary irony is the form's most concentrated argument: civilisation is not the cure for savagery — it is its more sophisticated form, the same anthropology operating at two scales at once. Golding's ending refuses the comfort of redemption: the boys are not saved from violence in Chapter 12; they are transferred from the small theatre of it to the large theatre of it, taken from a hand-held spear to a guided cruiser. The novella's closing tricolon "the darkness of man's heart" sits inside an ending that refuses to redeem that darkness — the structural argument is that the cruiser is the painted tribe's adult form, not its opposite. Thread this into: C1 Ralph / C8 Naval Officer / T1 Civilisation vs Savagery / T6 War / T10 Violence / any essay with a Chapter 12 conclusion.

3. The refused redemption — Golding's structural denial of catharsis.

Read together, the two counter-readings above produce a third: the novella refuses every redemptive frame the conventional Bildungsroman would supply. Ralph does not mature into authority — he is reduced to weeping prey. The boys are not rescued into safety — they are transferred to a larger theatre of the same violence. The painted face is not removed by the white-topped cap — it is replaced by it. Coral Island's Victorian myth that British boys placed in paradise reproduce Christian civility is named in the closing scene only to be refused: Golding's reader is asked to register the ending as the failure of that myth, not its fulfilment. This refused redemption is itself the novella's pedagogical move. The conventional reader expects the institution (the naval officer, the cruiser, the inherited authority of the adult uniform) to redeem the savagery the children have produced. Golding withholds the redemption to argue that the institution is the savagery's source, not its solution. Ralph weeps not because he is saved but because what he is being saved INTO is the planetary version of what he is being saved FROM. The novella's deepest move is the refusal of the moral resolution every previous British boys' adventure story had supplied. Thread this into: C1 Ralph / C2 Jack / T1 Civilisation vs Savagery / T3 Loss of Innocence / T6 War / T7 Responsibility / any conclusion.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's top-band descriptor asks for "a critical, exploratory, conceptualised response". A reading that locates the novella's argument at the level of twentieth-century industrialised violence — that specifies the cultural object (imperial-British public-school boys, not universal humanity) — and that traces the formal mechanism (the deflationary cruiser ending; the refused redemption) is the species of conceptualisation Examiner Reports name as Grade 9. Without it, a Roger essay is psychological case study; with it, the essay is post-Nuremberg political anatomy traced to its dramatic device.

A universal critical-angle method. Every text in the AQA spec rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a counter-reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together as the species of conceptualisation Grade 9 demands. For Lord of the Flies the dual readings are universal-anthropology/imperial-British-specificity, rescue-as-salvation/rescue-as-larger-theatre, redemption-arc/refused-redemption. Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on reader 1 — what the reader sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on reader 2 — what the reader recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author's purpose — what Golding may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

Lord of the Flies argues that civilisation is a thin veneer over an inherited human capacity for cruelty — that the savagery the boys produce on the island is not a regression to a primitive Other but the same human nature that produced Auschwitz and Hiroshima. Golding published the novella in 1954, with the death camps and the atomic destruction of Japan still inside living memory, against R.M. Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) — the Victorian myth that British boys placed in paradise will reproduce Christian civility. The naval officer's cruiser at the close belongs to the same Cold War whose nuclear evacuation produced the boys' plane crash in Chapter 1, and the deflationary "Like the Coral Island" completes Golding's structural argument that civilisation is not the cure for savagery but its more sophisticated form, the same anthropology operating at two scales at once.

Key terms:

• Allegory-as-fable — an allegorical narrative whose surface story carries a clear didactic argument about how human institutions or human nature behave; Golding's own term for the novella in his 1965 essay "Fable" (collected in The Hot Gates).

• Symbolic object — a concrete object that carries an abstract institutional or moral idea; the conch (parliamentary procedure), the signal fire (institutional hope of rescue), Piggy's spectacles (reason), the painted face (institutional disinhibition), the Lord of the Flies (the inherited capacity for cruelty).

• Free-indirect prose — narrative technique that slips between third-person narration and the character's interior thought; the closing "Ralph wept for the end of innocence, the darkness of man's heart, and the fall through the air of the true, wise friend called Piggy" (Chapter 12) collapses Ralph's interior into Golding's authorial verdict.

• Deflationary irony — an ending that resolves the narrative tension in a register lower than the tension demanded; the naval officer's "Like the Coral Island" (Chapter 12) names the Victorian source text Golding has spent the novella refuting.

• Refused coming-of-age — a coming-of-age arc that does not reach integration; Ralph reaches Chapter 12 not as a matured leader but as hunted prey weeping for what has been lost, the form's most direct case.

• Hamartia — the protagonist's error in action (Aristotle, Poetics); Ralph's three institutional failures (the fire let go, the assemblies dissolved, the unmade defence of Piggy) are his hamartia. NOT a fatal flaw in character.

• Beelzebub — the Hebrew name Lord of the Flies in translation; a Biblical demon of corruption, decay, and the institutional disorder of swarming insects. Golding's title locates the novella's argument in the religious-allegorical tradition that names the beast not on the mountain but in the boys.

Top 10 Character Questions

Most likely AQA Modern Text character questions, ordered by examiner-prediction confidence. Each carries one ready-to-rehearse plan with seven-sentence body paragraphs and an empty response area to draft against.

C1 — Ralph

Question

How does Golding present Ralph as a character whose authority declines across Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Ralph at this point in the novel

- how Golding uses Ralph to explore ideas about the decline of authority.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Ralph is the elected chief whose institutional authority declines in three registers — confident parliamentary inheritance in Chapter 1, eroding procedural grip across Chapters 5–8, hunted prey weeping under the naval officer's white-topped cap in Chapter 12. The plan tracks three stages of the same hamartia — election, erosion, prey — Golding's argument that institutional authority is not held in title but enacted in active defence, and that the elected chief's decline is the inheritance's decline registered in one boy.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The naval officer reading (see top of document). Ralph's transfer from hunted prey to rescued boy is not the institutional answer to his decline but its continuation under a larger uniform — the cruiser belonging to the same Cold War the boys had been fleeing. Land this in the conclusion at minimum.

Technique gloss:

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Symbolic object — the conch as parliamentary procedure; the white-topped cap.

• Hamartia — the protagonist's error in action (Ralph's three institutional failures).

• Tricolon — three parallel objects of grief in the closing sentence.

• Imperative declarative — the assembly's "the rules are the only thing we've got" (Chapter 5).

• Antithesis — election register against hunted-prey register.

• Hook (historical fact): Golding's notebooks for Lord of the Flies (Faber archive, drafted 1951–53) record that Ralph was the elected chief Golding had built as the inheritance's representative — the boy whose parliamentary instinct in Chapter 1 reaches for the conch "before he understands its weight", the same boy whose Chapter 12 weeping under the naval officer's cap collapses the elected chief into the hunted prey the painted tribe's manhunt has produced.

• Building sentence (historical context): Writing in 1953 with the H-bomb tested by the United States in 1952 and the Soviet Union in 1953 and the Belsen photographs of 1945 still inside the post-war reader's living memory, Golding constructs Ralph's authority not as a personal quality but as an institutional inheritance — democratic election, parliamentary procedure, civic discipline — whose decline he traces across twelve chapters in three institutional registers.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 1 election as the boy who carries the conch, the gradual dissolution of his procedural authority through Chapters 5–8 in which the painted tribe forms while Ralph still asks the right questions to an audience leaving the chamber, and his Chapter 12 collapse into hunted prey weeping under the naval officer's cap, Golding presents Ralph's decline as the inheritance's decline registered in one boy — institutional authority enacted in active defence rather than held in title, the elected chief's hamartia the inheritance's institutional warning to the post-war reader.

Scene focus: the Chapter 1 election; Ralph holding the conch; "the man with the conch"; the inheritance carried instinctively.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapter, Golding installs Ralph as the inherited chief — democratic election delivered by the symbolic object he has identified, parliamentary authority carried in a boy who has not yet had to defend the procedure that confers it.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys symbolic object inside free-indirect prose — Ralph holding the conch in Chapter 1 with the boys electing "the man with the conch" — combining symbolic object (the conch the institution made physical, parliamentary authority carried in a found shell rather than in any quality Ralph himself possesses) with free-indirect prose (the narration slipping between Ralph's emerging recognition of authority and the boys' collective decision, the elected chief produced by the inheritance rather than by the personal claim) inside the same opening scene.

• Close analysis: The definite article "the man" — converting a twelve-year-old boy into the institutional figure the conch authorises — registers the inheritance carrying the authority rather than Ralph generating it, while the prepositional phrase "with the conch" attaches the chief to the symbolic object the institution has supplied, the procedure preceding the person.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears Ralph's authority as institutional rather than personal — the conch the inheritance, the boy the inheritance's elected vessel, the election the procedure's first enactment.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the election as the institutional inheritance the British public-school plus Anglican-chapel architecture has supplied — parliamentary procedure carried as instinct rather than as choice — and registers Golding's argument that institutional authority in this society is inherited, not earned, and the inheritance the boys carry already contains the seed of its own collapse.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using Ralph's confident election to argue that institutional authority is inherited before it is enacted — the elected chief carrying the inheritance whose active defence the rest of the novella will reveal he has not been institutionally trained to enact under pressure.

• Context (AO3): The British public-school plus Anglican-chapel tradition had taught election and procedural rotation as civic instinct rather than as conscious decision; Ralph's Chapter 1 election is the inheritance carried unconsciously into the island, the institutional authority the form will track from confident election to dissolved chamber to hunted prey across twelve chapters.

Scene focus: the Chapter 5 assembly at dusk; Ralph's "the rules are the only thing we've got"; Jack's walk-out in Chapter 8; the procedural authority preserved in syntax but failing in attendance.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 1 had installed Ralph as the inheritance's confident chief, Chapters 5–8 stage the erosion of procedural authority — the elected chief unable to hold the parliamentary procedure against the painted tribe's pull, the procedural authority preserved in syntax but failing in the only register that institutionally matters, attendance.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys imperative declarative inside free-indirect prose — Ralph's Chapter 5 "the rules are the only thing we've got" set against Jack's Chapter 8 walk-out "I'm not going to play any longer. Not with you" — combining imperative declarative (Ralph's parliamentary appeal preserving the procedural inheritance in syntax even as the chamber's audience dissolves, the form of authority outlasting the substance) with antithesis (the parliamentary register "rules" set against the play-register "play any longer", the elected chief and the anti-protagonist registering the institutional argument in two registers of the same scene) inside the same arc.

• Close analysis: The definite article "the rules" — chosen against the more procedural "our rules" — registers the institutional principle as already abstract, the procedure preserved in syntax even as the assembly's attention dissolves, while the contraction "we've" still claims a collective the painted tribe is in the process of withdrawing from, the chief's syntax outlasting the chief's audience.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears institutional authority as the active defence of procedure under pressure — Ralph's appeal still articulate, the boys' attention still dissolving, the elected chief's authority preserved in form but failing in attendance.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the erosion as Ralph's hamartia extended — the second error in action of the same arc, the procedural defence the chief had agreed to and the chief is now unable to enact — and registers Golding's argument that institutional authority decays across many small moments of attention rather than in a single decisive collapse.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the dissolving assemblies to argue that institutional decline is iterative rather than catastrophic — the elected chief who cannot hold the procedural defence across Chapters 5–8 has already lost the discipline that would have prevented Jack's Chapter 8 walk-out, the small failures of attention enabling the larger institutional inversion.

• Context (AO3): The British public-school plus Anglican-chapel inheritance had taught parliamentary procedure as a civic discipline requiring active defence under contest; Ralph's failure to hold the procedural authority across Chapters 5–8 is the inheritance's institutional decline registered in the elected chief who cannot enact the discipline he had agreed to.

Scene focus: Chapter 12; Ralph hunted as "the beast" through the burning island; collapse at the naval officer's feet; the closing tricolon; the white-topped cap.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–8 had eroded Ralph's procedural authority, Chapter 12 stages the collapse of the elected chief into hunted prey — the inheritance's institutional authority reduced to a boy weeping under the naval officer's white-topped cap, the form's refused coming-of-age delivered in the closing tricolon's elegiac register.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys tricolon inside free-indirect prose — "Ralph wept for the end of innocence, the darkness of man's heart, and the fall through the air of the true, wise friend called Piggy" (Chapter 12) — combining tricolon (three parallel objects of grief building from the abstract "end of innocence" through the universal "darkness of man's heart" to the specific named friend "Piggy") with free-indirect prose (the narration fusing Ralph's grief with Golding's authorial verdict, the elected chief's interior collapse registered as the form's elegiac judgement on the inheritance) inside the same closing sentence.

• Close analysis: The verb "wept" — chosen against the more childlike "cried" — places Ralph in the elegiac register of adult mourning the refused coming-of-age requires, while the metaphor "the darkness of man's heart" converts the institutional consequence into the elegiac register the form has been preparing across twelve chapters, and the white-topped cap of the naval officer in the surrounding paragraph reframes the rescue as the institutional uniform of the same war the boys had been evacuated from.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the closing tricolon as the elected chief's institutional collapse made human — the abstract erosion of Chapters 5–8 landing in a single sentence of personal grief.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises Ralph as the protagonist of the refused coming-of-age — the elegiac recognition arriving only after the cost has been paid — and registers Golding's claim that the elected chief's decline is the inheritance's decline registered in one boy, and that the naval officer's white-topped cap is not the institutional alternative but the institutional continuation under uniform.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the closing tricolon and the white-topped cap together to argue that the elected chief's authority does not survive contact with the inheritance's institutional weakness — and that the post-war reader is being asked to recognise the cruiser at the close as the boys' painted tribe scaled up rather than as the inheritance restored.

• Context (AO3): John Carey's William Golding: The Man Who Wrote Lord of the Flies (2009) reads Ralph's closing collapse as the elected chief's hamartia in classical register — the error in action the protagonist makes, not the flaw he has, the inheritance's institutional decline registered in one boy's three institutional failures across the descent.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Golding traces Ralph's decline as the inheritance's institutional decline registered in one boy — confident election in Chapter 1, eroded procedural authority through Chapters 5–8, hunted prey weeping under the naval officer's white-topped cap in Chapter 12 — three stages of the same hamartia, institutional authority enacted in active defence rather than held in title.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 1 installed as inherited election, Chapters 5–8 eroded as the procedural authority the chief could not actively defend, Chapter 12 collapsed as the refused coming-of-age — three stages of the same decline, the elected chief's hamartia the inheritance's institutional warning the post-war reader is being asked to register.

• Author's purpose: Writing in the post-Belsen 1950s, Golding embodies in Ralph's decline the institutional warning the post-war reader was being asked to register — institutional authority active rather than titular, the elected chief's small failures of attention the inheritance's larger institutional collapse, and the cruiser at the close belonging to the same war whose inheritance the elected chief has not been able to defend.

• Ultimate moral: Ralph's enduring power as a fictional creation is that he is not the institution's enemy but its elected vessel — the boy whose inheritance had supplied the conch and whose hamartia had failed to defend it, the elected chief the post-war reader is being asked to recognise in every elected official whose procedural defence the inheritance will require under pressure.

C2 — Jack

Question

How does Golding present Jack as a character whose charisma becomes tyranny in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Jack at this point in the novel

- how Golding uses Jack to explore ideas about charismatic tyranny.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Jack is the anti-protagonist whose charisma becomes tyranny in three institutional registers — imperial confidence as head choirboy in Chapter 1, masked disinhibition under the painted face in Chapter 4, charismatic tyrant of the painted tribe at Castle Rock in Chapters 8–12. The plan tracks three stages of the same arc — imperial confidence, masked self, tribal tyranny — Golding's argument that charismatic authority is the institutional inversion's preferred form and the painted face the inheritance's permission for it.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The first non-negotiable (see top of document). Jack's painted-face tyranny is not the loss of European civilisation; it is European civilisation operating under altered institutional permission. The British public-school choirboy and the painted hunter are produced by the same architecture and the painted tribe is an institution in its own right, with its own ritual hierarchy. Land this in BP2 or BP3.

Technique gloss:

• Symbolic object — the painted face; the sharpened stick; Piggy's spectacles seized.

• Imperial declarative — Jack's "We're English; and the English are best at everything" (Chapter 2).

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping between Jack and the painted tribe's collective interior.

• Personification — the mask granted independent agency.

• Imperative chant — short rhythmic ritual speech ("Kill the pig").

• Single-clause verb (close analysis) — "liberated".

• Hook (historical fact): Golding's first description of Jack arrives in Chapter 1 with the choir "marching approximately in step in two parallel lines" under "black caps" and "long black cloaks" — the institutional uniform of the British Anglican-chapel choir Jack will paint over by Chapter 4, the boy whose inherited institutional discipline supplies the architecture his painted tribe will then invert under altered institutional permission.

• Building sentence (historical context): Writing in 1953 with the Nuremberg trials of 1945–46 having installed in post-war public consciousness the figure of the charismatic authoritarian — the ordinary citizen whose institutional permission produced extraordinary violence under uniformed sanction — Golding constructs Jack as the British public-school version of the same anatomy, the head choirboy whose charisma the painted face will then convert into the institutional tyranny the inheritance had supplied the architecture for.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 2 "We're English; and the English are best at everything", his Chapter 4 painting of the face that grants "liberation from shame and self-consciousness", and his Chapter 11 elevation at Castle Rock where the conch is destroyed by the executioner Jack has authorised, Golding uses Jack to argue that charismatic authority is the institutional inversion's preferred form — the painted face the inheritance's permission for the tyranny the chapel choir's discipline had been institutionally supplied to prevent.

Scene focus: Jack's Chapter 1 arrival with the choir; the Chapter 2 "We're English" speech; the head choirboy's institutional confidence.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs Jack as the head choirboy whose imperial confidence the inheritance has trained — the boy whose Anglican-chapel discipline supplies the institutional architecture the painted tribe will inherit, the "We're English; and the English are best at everything" speech the inheritance's imperial myth voiced precisely so the novella can refute it.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys imperial declarative inside symbolic object — Jack's Chapter 2 "We've got to have rules and obey them. After all, we're not savages. We're English; and the English are best at everything" delivered while the choir's "black caps" and "long black cloaks" remain visible — combining imperial declarative (the superlative "best at everything" the imperial myth voiced as institutional confidence) with symbolic object (the choir's uniform the Anglican-chapel architecture Jack inherits as the head choirboy of the institution that has trained him in discipline) inside the same opening assembly.

• Close analysis: The superlative "best at everything" — chosen against the merely "good at things" — registers the imperial confidence the inheritance has trained, while the negative "not savages" establishes the binary the painted face will then collapse, the Anglican-chapel discipline carried in syntax even as the institutional inversion is being prepared in the same boy's institutional position.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears Jack's confidence as inherited rather than personal — the head choirboy carrying the imperial myth the inheritance has trained him to voice, the discipline carried in the uniform he still wears.

C3 — Piggy

Question

How does Golding use Piggy to suggest ideas about reason and society in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Piggy at this point in the novel

- how Golding uses Piggy to suggest ideas about reason and society.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Piggy is reason embodied in a boy the institution has taught the others to mock — gatekeeper of parliamentary procedure in Chapters 1–4, increasingly desperate keeper of the conch through Chapters 5–9, killed at the moment he tries to reassert procedure at Castle Rock. The plan tracks three stages of the same disabling.

• Allegorical naming — Piggy as institutional epithet replacing a real name; the original name never given.

• Symbolic object — the conch as parliamentary procedure; the spectacles as reason in object form.

• Rhetorical question — Piggy's interrogative challenges to the tribe.

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Tricolon — three parallel imperatives or claims.

• Single-clause verb (close analysis) — "exploded".

• Hook (historical fact): Golding never tells the reader Piggy's real name — the institutional epithet imposed in the opening pages by the other boys, with Ralph's failed defence "He's not Fatty,' cried Ralph, 'his real name's Piggy!'" (Chapter 1), survives the entire novella and is still the only name in Ralph's closing tricolon.

• Building sentence (historical context): Piggy arrives in the opening pages with asthma, spectacles, plump build, and a working-class accent — every marker the British public-school tradition would set against him — and Golding constructs him as the institutional voice of reason in a body the inherited social architecture has already taught the other boys to mock.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 1 first identification of the conch and his suggestion of the meeting, his Chapter 11 final challenge "Which is better — to have rules and agree, or to hunt and kill?", and his death at Castle Rock in the same moment as the conch's destruction, Golding uses Piggy to argue that reason in this society is not innate strength but institutionally disabled — the voice that should be heard is the voice the institution has trained the others to ignore.

Scene focus: the opening pages; Piggy identifying the conch, suggesting the meeting, calling the roll, and being mocked.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs Piggy as the gatekeeper of parliamentary procedure — the boy who first identifies the conch and suggests the assembly — and the institutional disabling of reason as the corresponding inheritance, the voice that should be heard already trained out of the others' attention.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys allegorical naming inside ritual disabling — Ralph's failed defence "He's not Fatty,' cried Ralph, 'his real name's Piggy!'" (Chapter 1) immediately followed by the boys laughing — combining allegorical naming (the institutional epithet imposed by the group, the original name erased before the reader is given it) with structural irony (the boy who identifies the conch and reasons about the fire is the boy whose name the elected chief cannot defend) inside the same opening sequence.

• Close analysis: The reported verb "cried" — chosen against the more controlled "said" — registers Ralph's institutional weakness in real time, while the bare possessive "his real name" names a name the novella refuses to deliver, the institutional erasure carried in the absence the reader is left with.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional disabling of reason in the same scene as its identification — the boy whose voice should carry the parliamentary inheritance is the boy whose name the institution will never restore.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the cultural disabling of reason — the way inherited social architecture teaches a group to mock the very voice it most needs to hear — and registers Golding's claim that the institutional inheritance the boys carry already contains the seed of its own collapse.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the naming scene to argue that reason in this society is not naturally disabled but institutionally disabled — the others have been trained to mock Piggy by the same school life that taught them to elect Ralph, the inheritance bringing both the discipline and its disabling.

• Context (AO3): Golding had taught at Bishop Wordsworth's School in Salisbury from 1945 to 1962 and watched the British public-school inheritance in person; Piggy's mockery is the institutional disabling of reason Golding had observed in the inherited social architecture itself.

Scene focus: the broken assembly in Chapter 5; Piggy's increasingly desperate appeals to procedure as the tribe forms.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 1–4 had installed Piggy as gatekeeper of procedure, Chapters 5–9 stage the increasing desperation of his appeals — the conch's authority eroding as the painted tribe forms, reason still articulate but no longer institutionally heard.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys rhetorical question inside parliamentary appeal — Piggy's recurring "Which is better — to have rules and agree, or to hunt and kill?" (Chapter 11, anticipated through Chapters 5–9) — combining rhetorical question (the parliamentary form preserved even as the audience for it dissolves, reason's syntax surviving its institutional disabling) with antithesis ("rules and agree" set against "hunt and kill", the parliamentary inheritance set against the painted tribe's chosen alternative) inside the same recurring challenge.

• Close analysis: The interrogative form — "Which is better" — refuses to assert and instead invites the boys to choose, parliamentary procedure preserved even at the moment of its disabling, while the gerunds "to agree" and "to kill" convert two abstract nouns into two visible institutional acts.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises reason's institutional disabling as the precondition for the painted tribe's success — the voice that asks "which is better" still posing the right question, but to a group that has stopped answering — and registers Golding's argument that institutional disabling silences reason long before reason itself is killed.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using Piggy's increasingly desperate questions to argue that reason without institutional protection is rhetorical only — the parliamentary form survives its disabling but cannot reverse it, the asking itself no longer institutionally consequential.

• Context (AO3): The British public-school plus Anglican-chapel tradition taught parliamentary debate as a civic skill; Piggy's preserved rhetorical question is the inherited skill carried into a chamber that has elected to walk out, the institutional disabling visible in the absence of any audience to address.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–9 had made Piggy's appeals increasingly desperate, Chapter 11 stages the institutional argument made physical — Roger's boulder destroying both Piggy and the conch in the same moment, reason and parliamentary procedure killed in a single descending stone.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the institutional argument made physical — the moment the inherited capacity for cruelty has unmade the inherited capacity for reasoned dissent in the same sentence — and registers Golding's claim that reason without institutional defence is killed in the same descent that kills the institution.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the simultaneous destruction to argue that reason and parliamentary procedure are not separate inheritances but a single institutional architecture — when the conch is destroyed, Piggy is destroyed, because reason in this society has no shelter outside the institution that protected it.

• Context (AO3): Golding's Belsen-photograph generation had watched institutional violence on a continental scale; the simultaneous destruction of Piggy and the conch is the institutional argument the post-war reader is being asked to take into their own institutional life — reason disabled, then killed, in the same descent.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Golding uses Piggy to anatomise reason in this society as institutionally disabled rather than naturally weak — gatekeeper mocked in Chapter 1, increasingly desperate questioner through Chapters 5–9, killed with the conch at Castle Rock in Chapter 11.

• Controlling concept: What Chapters 1–4 installed as institutional disabling, Chapters 5–9 left as preserved rhetorical form, Chapter 11 destroyed in a single boulder — three stages of the same disabling, reason and parliamentary procedure killed together because they had always been a single institutional architecture.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1954, Golding embodies in Piggy the institutional disabling of reason he had watched the inherited social architecture produce — the boy whose real name is never given the institutional warning that the post-war reader is being asked to register before the next descent begins.

• Ultimate moral: Piggy's enduring power is that he is not the voice of weakness but the voice of disabling — reason articulate to the end, killed not by inferior argument but by an institutional architecture that elected to stop listening.

C4 — Roger

Question

How does Golding use the character of Roger to present ideas about cruelty in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Roger at this point in the novel

- how Golding uses Roger to present ideas about cruelty in the novel as a whole.

Frame. Roger's arm is the Belsen guard's arm — the same institutional executioner trained by the same inheritance to wait until the institutional permission arrives. Technique gloss:

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Symbolic object — the boulder; Piggy's spectacles; the conch.

• Periodic sentence — main clause delayed until the end, the inhibition named only at the close ("the taboo of the old life").

• Allegorical naming — Roger's surname unstated, the institutional epithet carried as bare given name.

• Single-clause verb (close analysis) — "leaned".

• Free-indirect interrogative — the boys' chant naming what Roger has already enacted.

• Hook (historical fact): Golding's notebooks for Lord of the Flies (Faber archive, drafted 1951–53) record that Roger was the last principal character he named — the figure he conceived as "the dark child" whose institutional disinhibition the novella would chart at the slowest speed, the boy whose arm is restrained in Chapter 4 by "the taboo of the old life" and who by Chapter 11 leans on a boulder "with a sense of delirious abandonment".

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 4 throwing of stones around — not at — the littluns with "a space round Henry, perhaps six yards in diameter, into which he dare not throw", his Chapter 8 elevation as the painted tribe's principal enforcer, and his Chapter 11 leaning on the lever that destroys both Piggy and the conch in the same moment, Golding uses Roger to argue that cruelty in this society is not an aberration but a capacity the institutional inheritance restrains and the painted tribe's order liberates — the institutional executioner the novella has been patiently preparing across the whole descent.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs Roger as the watched cruelty the institutional inheritance still restrains — the boy whose arm draws back to throw at the littluns but whose stones fall around rather than at them, the inherited inhibitions still holding even as the test for them has already begun.

• Close analysis: The single-clause verb "dare not throw" — with its modal "dare" converting the inhibition into a felt restraint Roger registers in his arm — names the institutional inheritance at the precise moment Roger has begun to test it, while the adjectives "invisible yet strong" convert the inheritance into a measurable architecture that the boy can feel without seeing.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the inheritance as a physical force on Roger's arm — the institutional inhibitions of school life and chapel life still holding the stones away from Henry by "six yards", the distance the boy can measure.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the test as the cruelty's first institutional rehearsal — the throwing-around-not-at the disinhibition Roger is preparing under the cover of the inheritance that still restrains him — and registers Golding's argument that institutional cruelty is built in this rehearsal phase, not in the executioner moment.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using Roger's Chapter 4 stones to argue that institutional cruelty is patiently built — the executioner's arm trained by tests the inheritance still restrains, the painted tribe's order arriving as the institutional permission Roger has already been rehearsing for.

• Context (AO3): The 1945 Nuremberg trials had installed in post-war public consciousness the figure of the institutional executioner whose cruelty was permitted by uniform; Golding's Roger throwing stones around Henry is the British public-school version of the same anatomy, the inheritance restraining the arm until the painted tribe's order replaces it.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the sharpened stick to argue that the executioner is the institution's product rather than the institution's enemy — Roger the boy the painted tribe has been waiting to liberate, the cruelty the inheritance restrained the cruelty the new institution authorises.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises Roger as the institutional figure the post-war reader had been asked to recognise — the executioner whose arm the previous institution had restrained and whose arm the new institution had liberated — and registers Golding's claim that the executioner is not the institution's enemy but its product, the cruelty the inheritance restrained the cruelty the inversion authorises.

• Controlling concept: What Chapters 1–4 restrained as "the taboo of the old life", Chapters 5–9 liberated through the painted tribe's ritual sanction, Chapter 11 enacted as "delirious abandonment" on the lever — three stages of the same arm, the institutional inheritance the executioner needs to lose before he can be liberated.

T5 — Group Mentality / Mob / Chant

Question

How does Golding explore how groups of boys behave together in Lord of the Flies?

- what different characters say and do

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Group behaviour in the novella is staged in three institutional registers — the elected parliamentary assembly under the conch in Chapters 1–4, the rehearsed hunt-dance and ritual chant in Chapters 5–8, the killing-mob that destroys Simon in Chapter 9. The plan tracks three stages of the same group anatomy — assembly, dance, mob — Golding's argument that the same boys produce three institutional behaviours under three institutional permissions, and that the killing-mob is not the absence of order but a different order's product.

Technique gloss:

• Imperative chant — short rhythmic ritual speech ("Kill the beast! Cut his throat!").

• Trochaic stress pattern — heavy-light syllabic foot driving the chant's momentum.

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the collective interior of the group.

• Personification — the mob granted a single grammatical agent ("the circle", "the tribe").

• Anaphora — repeated opening phrase across the chant's three lines.

• Demonstrative pronoun (close analysis) — "the thing" in the dance.

• Hook (historical fact): Gustave Le Bon's The Crowd: A Study of the Popular Mind (1895) had given the post-1945 generation the framework for understanding the killing-mob — the argument that the individual conscience dissolves in the collective and the group acts in a register no individual would authorise alone — and Golding's chant "Kill the beast! Question

Frame. The adult war frames the novella at three points — the opening evacuation in Chapter 1 with its passing reference to "the atom bomb", the dead parachutist drifting onto the mountain in Chapter 6 as the war's intrusion onto the island, and the naval officer's cruiser arriving in Chapter 12 belonging to the same war the boys had been fleeing. The plan tracks three stages of the same frame — distant cause, intruding evidence, deflationary verdict — Golding's argument that the boys' violence and the adult war's violence are not different in kind, only in scale.

• Frame narrative — the boys' island violence framed by the adult war that produced their plane crash and the cruiser that retrieves them.

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Hook (historical fact): Golding finished Lord of the Flies in 1953, nine years after returning from Royal Navy service that had taken him through the sinking of the Bismarck, the D-Day landings, and the Belsen liberation photographs of 1945 — and built the novella as a deliberate frame in which the boys' island violence on a Pacific scale is bracketed at both ends by the adult war on a planetary scale.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs the adult war as the boys' distant cause — the evacuation that has brought them to the island, the "atom bomb" that has killed the adults they had been travelling with, the "scar" the plane has left as the war's visible trace on the island's interior.

T7 — Responsibility

Question

How does Golding explore ideas about responsibility in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Golding presents ideas about responsibility.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Responsibility in the novella is staged as Ralph's three institutional failures — the signal fire let go in Chapter 4 because the assembly had been distracted by the hunt, the gradual dissolution of the parliamentary assemblies through Chapters 5–8, and the moment Ralph fails to defend Piggy in Chapter 11 at Castle Rock. The plan tracks three stages of the same hamartia — the error in action the protagonist makes, not the flaw he has — Golding's argument that responsibility is institutional rather than individual and that the elected chief's failures are the inheritance's failures registered in one boy.

Technique gloss:

• Hamartia — the protagonist's error in action (not a fatal flaw); Ralph's institutional failures in three registers.

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Symbolic object — the signal fire as institutional hope; the conch as parliamentary procedure.

• Imperative declarative — the assembly's "the rules are the only thing we've got" (Chapter 5).

• Modal verb (close analysis) — "should have" / "could have".

• Tricolon — Ralph's three failures across the descent.

• Hook (historical fact): Aristotle's Poetics (c. 335 BCE) had defined hamartia as the protagonist's error in action — not a fatal flaw in character but a decision the protagonist takes whose consequences the rest of the tragedy traces — and Ralph's three institutional failures across Lord of the Flies (the fire let go, the assemblies that dissolve, the moment he fails to defend Piggy) constitute his hamartia in the Aristotelian register the novella inherits.

Three context factors that travel across all 20 questions

Every essay should thread one of these into one BP. Pick the factor whose period-fit is sharpest for the Q.

1. Golding's Royal Navy service 1940–45 + the Belsen liberation photographs (1945) + the savagery-is-the-same-human-nature reading. Best fit: C1 Ralph, C2 Jack, C3 Piggy, C4 Roger, C5 Simon, T1 Civilisation vs Savagery, T2 Inherent Evil, T3 Loss of Innocence, T5 Group Mentality, T7 Responsibility, T8 Fear, T10 Violence. Why it lands: Golding's 1959 remark "I was very impressed with what people are capable of doing" and his statement that Lord of the Flies is "an attempt to trace the defects of society back to the defects of human nature" name the institutional collapse the boys' island miniaturises; the painted tribe, Simon's killing, and Roger's boulder are the institutional pattern Golding had watched adult institutions produce on a continental scale. The defects he had in mind are the defects he had seen the most civilised nations of Europe enact across the previous decade — Auschwitz, Hiroshima, Dresden, Belsen — and the boys' savagery is the same human nature given altered ritual permission rather than regression to a primitive Other.

2. The Cold War + the nuclear arms race + the H-bomb tests (US 1952 / USSR 1953 / UK 1957) + the naval-officer reading. Best fit: C8 Naval Officer, T4 Symbolism, T5 Group Mentality, T6 War, T1 Civilisation vs Savagery, C6 Lord of the Flies, T10 Violence. Why it lands: the cruiser at the close belongs to the same Cold War institutional order the boys' plane crash was fleeing; the institutional argument scales from boys to adults in the same anthropology operating at two scales at once. The 1952 US H-bomb test and the 1953 Soviet H-bomb test had installed in post-war public consciousness the cruiser standing offshore as the institutional vehicle of the credible threat of mutual destruction, and the naval officer's white-topped cap is the institutional uniform of that war — the rescuer arriving from the very same war the boys' descent has replicated in miniature.

3. R.M. Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) + the Victorian myth of British boys' adventure + Golding's deliberate subversion of the imperialist boys'-adventure tradition. Best fit: T1 Civilisation vs Savagery, T2 Inherent Evil, T3 Loss of Innocence, T6 War, T9 Rules and Order, C1 Ralph, C2 Jack, C8 Naval Officer, C10 Choir. Why it lands: Ballantyne's three British boys (Ralph, Jack, Peterkin) reproduce Christian civility on the Pacific island; Golding chose the same names and the same setting precisely to make the inversion unmissable. The Victorian boys'-adventure tradition (Ballantyne 1858, Captain Marryat's Masterman Ready 1841, R.L. Stevenson's Treasure Island 1883) had told generations of British children that the British public-school inheritance produces civility under any institutional pressure; Golding's painted tribe, Simon's killing, and the naval officer's "Like the Coral Island" (Chapter 12) refute the tradition at the level of its constituent assumptions, the metafiction the form's most direct technical move.

Plus two further factors for the new questions:

4. The 1945–46 Nuremberg trials + Hannah Arendt's Origins of Totalitarianism (1951) + Arendt's Eichmann in Jerusalem (1963) on institutional perpetrator responsibility. Best fit: C4 Roger, C7 Sam and Eric, T5 Group Mentality, T7 Responsibility, T10 Violence. Why it lands: the trials installed in post-war public consciousness the figure of the institutional executioner whose cruelty was permitted by uniform — Roger's arm restrained by "the taboo of the old life" and then liberated by the painted tribe's sanction is the British public-school version of the same anatomy. Arendt's later work on the banality of evil (1963) would name what Golding had already staged in 1954 — the ordinary citizen whose institutional participation in mass violence is sustained by the institutional sanction the architecture supplies.

5. Le Bon's The Crowd: A Study of the Popular Mind (1895) + Jung's Two Essays on Analytical Psychology (1928) on collective conscience and projection. Best fit: T5 Group Mentality, T8 Fear, C5 Simon. Why it lands: Le Bon had argued that the individual conscience dissolves in the collective and the group acts in a register no individual would authorise alone — the painted tribe's chant is the public-school inheritance staging Le Bon's anatomy in twelve-year-old form. Jung had argued that the individual projects the shadow's capacity for cruelty onto an external object the conscious self can then fear and disown — the boys' "snake-thing" and imagined beast are the British public-school version of that projection.

Two critical voices that travel across all 20 questions

Every essay should name one critic, with year, in one BP or in the conclusion. Pick the voice whose claim is sharpest for the Q.

1. John Carey, William Golding: The Man Who Wrote Lord of the Flies (2009). The authoritative biography reads Golding's career-long institutional argument as continuous with his Royal Navy service — argues that Lord of the Flies is the dramatic distillation of Golding's wartime experience converted into post-war fable, the closing tricolon the elegiac registration of the institutional collapse the Belsen photographs had asked the post-war reader to register, and the cruiser at the close the institutional inversion the form has been preparing across twelve chapters. Best fit: C1 Ralph (the elegiac arc), C4 Roger (the executioner's arm), C5 Simon (the prophet killed), C8 Naval Officer (the cruiser inversion), T1 Civilisation vs Savagery (the deflationary cruiser), T2 Inherent Evil (the elegiac verdict), T3 Loss of Innocence (the closing tricolon), T6 War (the war frame), T8 Fear (the projection enacted), T9 Rules and Order (the institutional architecture), C6 Lord of the Flies (the entity's verdict in elegiac register), CP-A Ralph and Jack (the elegiac arc).

2. Stephen Medcalf, William Golding (Writers and Their Work, 1975). Reads the novella as a religious-allegorical fable in the tradition of Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress (1678) and Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) — argues that the symbolic objects (the conch, the painted face, the Lord of the Flies) deliver the institutional argument the realist surface alone could not authorise, and that the religious-allegorical naming (Simon as apostle, Lord of the Flies as Beelzebub) locates the argument in the post-war fable tradition's chief argumentative inheritance. Best fit: C2 Jack (the painted face), C3 Piggy (reason institutionally disabled), C4 Roger (the institutional executioner), C5 Simon (religious-allegorical naming), C6 Lord of the Flies (religious-allegorical naming as structural centre), C10 Choir (the institutional architecture preserved across the descent), T2 Inherent Evil (religious-allegorical recognition), T4 Symbolism (the form's chief technical move), T7 Responsibility (Ralph's hamartia in classical register), T8 Fear (the Lord of the Flies' "I'm part of you" as structural centre), T10 Violence (the simultaneous destruction at Castle Rock), CP-B Simon (religious-allegorical naming), CP-D Leadership (the institutional dialectic).
